metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile verifier context-limit recovery: decide whether a single oversized PrerequisiteVerificationBlock's findings are (a) split into halves across separate calls (Binding workflow) or (b) treated as an unsplittable singleton that escalates through models_at_or_above() then emits indeterminate/evaluation-error (Binding focused prompt), and state the chosen algorithm once in a single authoritative location.
evidence: 'Focused verifier context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm as the finder: split the stable finding-record list into contiguous halves, retry independently, and escalate a singleton through models_at_or_above().'
'Recursive recovery may split an oversized record's finding tuple, but never splits subject or prerequisite plan text.'
findings.finding: The verifier context-limit recovery algorithm is contradicted between two sections: 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts' specifies splitting the finding-record list into contiguous halves (between PrerequisiteVerificationBlock records), while 'Binding workflow production and verifier batching' specifies that recursive recovery may split an oversized record's finding tuple (within a single PrerequisiteVerificationBlock). These are different splitting strategies for the same scenario and would send an implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Implementation ('Focused verifier context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm…') vs Binding workflow ('Recursive recovery may split an oversized record's finding tuple, but never splits subject or prerequisite plan text.')
scenarios: Implementer reads Binding focused prompt and implements record-list halving; later reads Binding workflow and implements intra-record tuple splitting — two incompatible recovery paths for the same failure mode.
A single PrerequisiteVerificationBlock whose findings tuple is too large: Binding focused prompt says escalate through models_at_or_above(); Binding workflow says split the tuple. Different outcomes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define how `relation_snapshot` is delivered from the `precheck` step to `ProductionBatchRunner`: either extend `BatchRunRequest` with a `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any]` field and update `interpreter.py::_run_batch` to populate it from `step.get('with', {})` resolved against bindings, OR document an alternative delivery mechanism (e.g. reading from `rc.workflow` context or a separate mediating `uses:` op) — the current `with:` key on a `batch:` step is not read by the interpreter.
evidence: Plan quote: 'precheck exposes the single upstream `relation_snapshot`; `finders` receives it in `with:` alongside routed criteria'
`BatchRunRequest` dataclass has exactly: finder, criteria, usd_budget, model_ladder, workflow, target_ticket, repo_root, run_id, step_id — no with_inputs field
`_run_batch` in interpreter.py builds BatchRunRequest from `batch.get('prompt')`, `batch.get('criteria')`, `batch.get('model_ladder')` only — no `step.get('with')` read
production_batch_runner.py docstring itself notes 'The v3 `batch` schema is immutable' (D5)
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for delivering `relation_snapshot` from the `precheck` step to the `ProductionBatchRunner` via a `with:` key on the `batch:` YAML step does not work: `interpreter.py::_run_batch` never reads `step.get('with')` or `batch.get('with')`, and `BatchRunRequest` has no field for extra with-inputs. The plan states 'precheck exposes the single upstream `relation_snapshot`; finders receives it in `with:` alongside routed criteria' but this delivery channel simply does not exist in the current batch-step infrastructure.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Binding workflow production and verifier batching': 'precheck exposes the single upstream relation_snapshot; finders receives it in with: alongside routed criteria'; also `src/rebar/llm/workflow/interpreter.py::_run_batch` (lines 351-399) and `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py::BatchRunRequest` (lines 61-74)
scenarios: At implementation time, adding `relation_snapshot: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.relation_snapshot }}` to the `finders` batch `with:` block in the YAML would have no effect — `BatchRunRequest` never receives it, `ProductionBatchRunner.run(req)` cannot access it.

The prerequisite-consistency criterion would be enabled by `assemble_criteria` but `ProductionBatchRunner` would have no snapshot to route through `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage`, causing silent failure or a runtime KeyError.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `BatchRunRequest` with `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)` and add to `_run_batch`: `with_inputs = {k: resolve_value(v, rc, prefixes, bindings) for k, v in (step.get('with') or {}).items()}` before building `BatchRunRequest`. Then `ProductionBatchRunner.run(req)` can access `req.with_inputs.get('relation_snapshot')` to retrieve the preload.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether DET-floor blocking findings should be exempted from the prerequisite-indeterminate clearing step: either explicitly exclude findings with `tier='DET'` from the copy-and-clear (so `parts["blocking"]` retains DET blocks and the verdict remains BLOCK even when prerequisite coverage is indeterminate), or add a rationale for why DET blocks should also be demoted.
evidence: Plan quote: 'copy every entry from `parts["blocking"]` into `parts["indeterminate"]` with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, then clear `parts["blocking"]`'
DET P1/P5/P8 findings arrive in `parts["blocking"]` via `orchestrator.partition_findings(det_blocks, det_advisories, decided, ...)` — they are not LLM-derived
orchestrator.py `finalize_verdict` already handles INDETERMINATE independently for LLM-tier failures; DET blocks produce BLOCK regardless of LLM availability
ABSENCE of rationale in the plan for why deterministic structural blocks should be cleared on a separate LLM failure
findings.finding: The plan's verdict-mutation step — copying all entries from `parts["blocking"]` to `parts["indeterminate"]` and clearing `parts["blocking"]` when any prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate — suppresses deterministic DET-floor P1/P5/P8 blocking findings because of an unrelated LLM prerequisite-evaluation failure (e.g. a provider timeout or invalid output). DET blocks are structural, model-independent findings; demoting them to INDETERMINATE because a focused LLM call failed conflates two independent failure domains. No rationale is given for why a DET P8-too-big block should be suppressed when a prerequisite snapshot evaluation times out.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'This story explicitly modifies both decision assembly and terminal verdict': 'if any final prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate, copy every entry from `parts["blocking"]` into `parts["indeterminate"]` with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, then clear `parts["blocking"]`'
scenarios: A provider rate-limit or invalid JSON response on the focused prerequisite finder maps all unresolved IDs to `indeterminate/evaluation-error`. The plan then clears all DET blocks (including a P8-too-big structural block) to INDETERMINATE, returning an unsigned INDETERMINATE instead of a BLOCK on a known-real structural violation.
An operator fixes a DET P5 cycle block and retries — but the prerequisite provider is down — so the gate issues INDETERMINATE instead of BLOCK, masking the real blocking issue from remediation UX.
findings.suggested_fix: Limit the clearing step to non-DET LLM findings: `det_still_blocking = [f for f in parts['blocking'] if f.get('tier') == 'DET']`; move only the LLM-sourced blocking findings to `parts['indeterminate']`; retain `parts['blocking'] = det_still_blocking`. If DET blocks exist, set verdict to BLOCK; if prerequisite is also indeterminate, surface INDETERMINATE as a secondary signal in coverage rather than suppressing the primary BLOCK.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the behavior when the `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload itself fails (e.g., a transient provider error or network failure before the preload completes). The plan only describes what happens when the preload succeeds with no readable prerequisites (route does not apply → `has_prerequisites=false`, empty arrays) and when individual prerequisite evaluations fail (evaluation-error/output-invalid → indeterminate records). A preload failure is a distinct case: the preload was attempted but errored before producing any snapshot. If this silently maps to `has_prerequisites=false`, a transient upstream failure causes the prerequisite consistency criterion to be skipped entirely without any indeterminate record in the verdict—the review PASSes without having checked prerequisite consistency, and the implementer has no signal that the check was skipped. This is a fail-open gap for a criterion the plan registers as `default_posture: blocking`.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'It runs only when the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload succeeded and contains readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites'; also Upstream contract section; Acceptance Criteria: 'Every readable pinned prerequisite receives exactly one closed-schema disposition'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism (e.g., a feature flag or operator kill switch) for the prerequisite-indeterminate→INDETERMINATE verdict path so the focused prerequisite route can be disabled without a code deploy if it produces spurious indeterminate records at scale.
evidence: 'if any final prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate, copy every entry from parts["blocking"] into parts["indeterminate"] with decision=indeterminate'
'In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, add if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE" before existing verdict branches'
No feature flag, kill switch, or rollback sequence is mentioned anywhere in the plan
Binding comparative baseline section says only 'existing verdicts remain unchanged' for P8/drift_refresh, but does not address rollback of the new path itself
findings.finding: The behavioral changes to `finalize_verdict` (new INDETERMINATE branch before existing elif chain) and `plan_review_decide` (blocker-to-indeterminate reclassification when any prerequisite record is indeterminate) have no rollback or back-out mechanism described. If the focused finder/verifier route produces spurious evaluation-error indeterminate records at scale (e.g., due to provider instability), every plan review would be forced to INDETERMINATE with no way to disable the path without a code deploy.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'In plan_review_decide, after the single partition_findings call…' and 'In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, add if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate")…'
scenarios: Provider is temporarily unstable; focused finder calls all return evaluation-error; every plan review reaching the prerequisite path is forced to INDETERMINATE with no override mechanism
A bug in prerequisite record parsing causes spurious indeterminate dispositions; production is blocked until a code fix is deployed
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a configuration flag (e.g., `PREREQUISITE_COVERAGE_ENABLED: bool = True` in a feature-flags module or workflow YAML parameter) that, when false, causes `ProductionBatchRunner` to return an empty `prerequisite_coverage` array and `plan_review_decide` to skip the indeterminate-promotion logic entirely. Document the flag as the back-out path in this plan's Implementation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change 'implements the exact `_build_binary(..., extra_fields=...)` and `_build_verification_output(..., binary_extra_fields=...)` signatures' to 'must be changed to accept the exact ... signatures' to accurately reflect that these are forward edits, not existing signatures.
evidence: Plan text: 'Explicit kernel edit target: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` implements the exact `_build_binary(..., extra_fields=...)` and `_build_verification_output(..., binary_extra_fields=...)` signatures defined in Implementation'
verify_models.py line 91: `def _build_binary(forbid: Any) -> type:` — no `extra_fields` parameter
verify_models.py line 108: `def _build_verification_output(forbid: Any, severity_cls: type) -> type:` — no `binary_extra_fields` parameter
ABSENCE: no grep hit for `extra_fields` or `binary_extra_fields` in the review_kernel directory
findings.finding: The 'Binding criterion and CLI registration' section says 'Explicit kernel edit target: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` implements the exact `_build_binary(..., extra_fields=...)` and `_build_verification_output(..., binary_extra_fields=...)` signatures' — present-tense 'implements' asserts these signatures already exist. They do not: the file shows `_build_binary(forbid)` (line 91) and `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls)` (line 108), neither of which has the stated keyword arguments.
findings.location: Binding criterion and CLI registration — 'Explicit kernel edit target' paragraph
scenarios: An implementer reads 'implements the exact ... signatures' and concludes the signatures are already there and only need to be called — skipping the actual signature-change work, causing a runtime TypeError when `plan_review_verification_model` tries to pass `binary_extra_fields`.
A reviewer checking the AC 'code-review schemas remain unchanged' cannot verify it without first understanding that both functions need new kwargs — an ambiguity the present-tense wording obscures.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace: 'implements the exact `_build_binary(..., extra_fields=...)` and `_build_verification_output(..., binary_extra_fields=...)` signatures defined in Implementation'
With: 'must be edited to accept the exact `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields=...)` and `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls, *, binary_extra_fields=...)` signatures defined in Implementation; both currently lack these kwargs.'

evidence: description is 28831 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete file-integrity proving command for AC3, such as `git diff --exit-code src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-container.eval.yaml`, to verify the tolerance values are actually unchanged, not merely that the eval passes within potentially-widened tolerances.
evidence: 'require both exit 0 and the existing tolerance values in plan-review-container.eval.yaml to remain unchanged'
The proving command is: 'run .venv/bin/python -m rebar prompt eval plan-review-container and .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_evals.py -k container; require both exit 0'

No git diff or jq assertion on the YAML file is included — exit 0 is necessary but not sufficient to prove tolerances are unchanged (verify-command defect: fixture invalidity / missing cardinality assertion).
findings.finding: AC3 states 'Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results,' and the proving command requires that 'the existing tolerance values in `plan-review-container.eval.yaml` to remain unchanged.' However, the proving command (`rebar prompt eval plan-review-container` + pytest) does not include a concrete diff or structured assertion that the YAML tolerance file itself is byte-identical. The eval runner's exit code proves the eval passed within tolerances but does NOT prove the tolerance values were not quietly loosened to accommodate a regression.
findings.location: AC line 3 ('Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results'); Binding comparative baseline compatibility section proving command
scenarios: A developer who widens a tolerance value to make the eval pass would satisfy the 'exit 0' proving command while violating the AC. The AC would appear green while the regression is hidden.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC3 proving command: `git diff --exit-code src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-container.eval.yaml && echo 'tolerances unchanged'`. This exits nonzero if the file was modified, making the check deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that when a focused verifier call fails with a non-context-limit error after retry exhaustion, every canonical_id whose local finding indices were included in that batch is marked indeterminate/evaluation-error (mirroring the finder's 'every unresolved ID' rule).
evidence: 'Non-context provider failure emits that record for every unresolved ID' — stated for finder path
Verifier failure handling references 'context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm as the finder' but is silent on non-context provider failure granularity for the verifier
The verifier result is index-keyed (0..P-1) whereas the finder result is canonical_id-keyed; the mapping from a failed verifier batch back to canonical_ids requires an explicit rule
findings.finding: The plan specifies that non-context provider failure causes the finder to emit indeterminate/evaluation-error 'for every unresolved ID', but does not state the equivalent failure-attribution rule for the focused verifier when a mid-batch verifier call fails with a non-context error. The verifier operates on PrerequisiteVerificationBlock records grouped by canonical_id; it is unclear whether a verifier batch failure marks all canonical_ids in that bin as indeterminate or only the ones whose index entries were not returned.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'The focused verifier…emits the existing {index, severity_attributes, binary} entry' and 'Non-context provider failure emits that record for every unresolved ID'; also Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — 'Focused finder and verifier calls use the existing runner retry classification'
scenarios: A verifier batch covering three prerequisites fails with a 429-exhausted-retries error; without a stated rule the implementation could mark all three indeterminate, only the first, or raise an unhandled exception
findings.suggested_fix: In the Binding focused prompt and failure contracts section, add: 'On non-context provider failure after retry exhaustion, the verifier marks every canonical_id represented in the failed bin as indeterminate/evaluation-error, identical to the finder rule for unresolved IDs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `test_upstream_contract` must be written as a standalone first micro-step (before any other focused-route code) and add it explicitly as Step 0 in the Binding implementation sequence, or acknowledge that the test is delivered by an upstream story and reference the file path it already populates.
evidence: 'At implementation start run .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py -k upstream_contract'

The file tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py is listed as a new file receiving new tests throughout the plan (test_largest_window_exhaustion_emits_indeterminate, observability tests, etc.) — it does not pre-exist.
No step in the implementation sequence says 'first, write only the upstream_contract test' before other tests are added to the file.
findings.finding: The Binding prerequisite block and verifier prompt section instructs: 'At implementation start run `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py -k upstream_contract` to assert the merged PlanRelationSnapshot fields and execution-review call signature match this plan before any focused-route code is changed.' However, `test_plan_review_prerequisites.py` is a NEW file that this very story creates (it is named throughout as the home for new prerequisite tests). The upstream_contract test cannot be run at implementation start if it does not yet exist, creating a circular dependency: the test must be written before it can be run, but writing it is part of the implementation that it is supposed to gate.
findings.location: Binding prerequisite block and verifier prompt section — last paragraph; also Implementation section 'At implementation start, verify both symbols and contract tests exist'
scenarios: An executor following the binding implementation sequence literally cannot run the upstream_contract test at implementation start because the file does not exist yet — they must either write the test first (undocumented step) or skip the gate check, undermining the upstream-drift guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Step 0 in the Binding implementation sequence: 'Write only `test_upstream_contract` in `tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py` (no other test stubs), run it, and confirm it passes before proceeding to Step 1.' Alternatively, if the upstream stories 75de/5967 already deliver this test, name the file path they deliver it in.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit acceptance criterion covering the `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` structured event: that every evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits the event with all required fields (ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, bin size), that counters are partitioned by reason code, and that no event contains prerequisite plan text.
evidence: 'Emit structured event plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size for every evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid record. Increment counters partitioned by reason code; never include prerequisite plan text.'
ABSENCE: no AC line reads 'observability event', 'plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate', 'counter', or 'partitioned by reason code'.

'Add acceptance coverage for observability: tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size; counters are partitioned by reason; captured events contain no prerequisite plan text.'
findings.finding: The structured observability deliverable — emitting `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` events with specific fields (ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, bin size) and incrementing counters partitioned by reason code — is described in Implementation and Binding operational behavior, with test assertions required in Binding focused prompt, but has no corresponding acceptance criterion. This is described work with no covering AC.
findings.location: AC section (no covering line) vs 'Binding operational behavior' ('Emit structured event plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate…') vs 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts' ('Add acceptance coverage for observability: tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits…')
scenarios: An implementer could skip event emission or omit required fields (e.g. bin size) since no AC gates on it — the verification commands do not include the observability test file explicitly in the required run.
A reviewer checking AC completeness would not flag a missing observability implementation because no AC maps to it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC list: '- [ ] Every evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid prerequisite record emits a `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` structured event with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size; counter increments are partitioned by reason code; no event payload contains prerequisite plan text.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the three novel architectural decisions introduced here: (a) dual non-overlapping finding index domains, (b) prerequisite material isolation invariant, and (c) the prerequisite-indeterminate → forced INDETERMINATE verdict signal chain, so future contributors have a maintained reference beyond this ticket.
evidence: 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains. General arrays retain 0..G-1. Flatten focused findings in stable (prerequisite_id, finding_index) order into local 0..P-1'

'Define measurable isolation: capture every request and assert that each general finder, general verifier, and coach system prompt, user prompt, and instructions contain neither prerequisite canonical IDs nor prerequisite plan text'
'In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, add if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE" before existing verdict branches, with existing branches changed to elif'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions — a dual index domain (general 0..G-1 vs focused 0..P-1) with non-overlapping verification arrays, a new verdict signal chain (prerequisite indeterminate → INDETERMINATE forced before existing elif), isolation invariants (no prerequisite material in general prompts), and a new pipeline stage (focused finder/verifier with independent packing) — but mentions no update to an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design document. For a change of this scope crossing passes.py, sizing.py, verify_models.py, plan-review.yaml, workflow_ops.py, orchestrator.py, parity.py, and multiple schemas, the absence of an architectural record risks future contributors re-introducing coupling the isolation invariants are designed to prevent.
findings.location: Implementation section (dual index domain, finalize_verdict change, isolation invariants); Binding workflow production and verifier batching; Binding signal-chain and identity tests
scenarios: A future contributor adds a convenience shortcut that passes prerequisite IDs to a general finder, violating isolation invariants, because the invariant and its rationale are only in a ticket plan rather than a maintained design document
Index domain accounting is subtly changed when a new finding type is added, because the dual-domain invariant is not captured in an architectural record accessible to that contributor

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the numeric values of `PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS`, `DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM`, and `VERIFY_SYSTEM_RESERVE_TOKENS` in the plan (or name the story/file that already defines them), so the executor has no design decision to make.
evidence: 'per_block_output_tokens: int = PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS'
'headroom: float = DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM'
'Compute fixed input as est_tokens(system_prompt) + est_tokens(subject_plan) + VERIFY_SYSTEM_RESERVE_TOKENS'
No section in the plan defines or links these constants; no upstream story is cited as their source.
findings.finding: The sizing.py `pack_prerequisite_bins` and `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` specs reference three constants — `PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS`, `DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM`, and `VERIFY_SYSTEM_RESERVE_TOKENS` — as defaults and inputs without stating their values or identifying which prior story introduces them. If any is absent from the codebase at implementation start, the executor has no stated default to use.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 4 (`pack_prerequisite_bins` definition); Binding workflow production and verifier batching (`pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` definition)
scenarios: An executor who checks sizing.py and finds these constants absent must choose values — a real design decision the plan does not make.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'These reuse the existing constants defined in `sizing.py` introduced by story 75de: `PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS = 300`, `DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM = 0.85`, `VERIFY_SYSTEM_RESERVE_TOKENS = 512`.' Adjust values to match whatever the upstream story specifies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace every `.venv/bin/python` invocation in Verification, Binding implementation sequence, Binding signal-chain tests, and Binding focused prompt contracts with a platform-neutral form (e.g. `python -m pytest` with an activated venv, or a `make`/`just` target that resolves the interpreter portably) so the commands execute on Windows as well as POSIX platforms.
evidence: ".venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review.py ..."
".venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing ..."
".venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py -k prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain"
On Windows, PEP 405 virtualenvs place the interpreter at .venv\Scripts\python.exe; .venv/bin/ does not exist under Windows venv layout.
rebar's supported platform matrix includes Windows (developer workstations and CI).
findings.finding: Every verification and proving command in the plan uses `.venv/bin/python` (a POSIX virtual-environment path). On Windows, the Python interpreter in a venv lives at `.venv\Scripts\python.exe`; the POSIX path does not exist and cannot be resolved by the shell.
findings.location: Verification section — every `.venv/bin/python` invocation; also repeated in 'Binding implementation sequence' first-slice and second-slice proving commands, 'Binding signal-chain and identity tests', and 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts'.
scenarios: Windows developer workstation or Windows CI: running `.venv/bin/python -m pytest ...` fails immediately with 'The system cannot find the path specified' or equivalent, making every verification and proving step in the plan unreachable on that platform.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `.venv/bin/python` with `python` (assuming the venv is activated before running verification commands, which is standard practice) or use a cross-platform runner target such as `make test-prerequisites` that resolves the interpreter via `$(PYTHON)` or equivalent. Document the venv-activation prerequisite once rather than embedding POSIX paths in every command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract a generic `_greedy_pack_bins(blocks, fixed_tokens, charge_fn, limit)` inner helper in `sizing.py` and delegate both `pack_prerequisite_bins` and `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` to it, the same way `verify_request_chunks` was extracted to the kernel.
evidence: `pack_container_bins` (sizing.py:48): fixed-input + greedy per-child charge + (bins, oversized) return — identical logic

Plan specifies `pack_prerequisite_bins`: 'Compute fixed input as est_tokens(system_prompt) + est_tokens(subject_plan) + VERIFY_SYSTEM_RESERVE_TOKENS. Charge each whole block... Greedily append while... return a block as oversized when...'
Plan specifies `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` with the same structure: 'Sort by canonical ID. Use the same fixed-input calculation as finder packing; charge each whole record...'
NIH: the same algorithm already exists twice over (finder + verifier variants) without extraction
findings.finding: `pack_prerequisite_bins` in `sizing.py` duplicates the greedy bin-packing algorithm already implemented by `pack_container_bins` in the same file — same fixed-input calculation, same per-block charge, same greedy-append loop, same (bins, oversized) return shape. The plan then adds a second duplicate `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` with the same structure. Neither extracts a shared generic inner packer.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define `pack_prerequisite_bins`...' and 'Binding workflow: Define `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins`...' / `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sizing.py` lines 48-76
scenarios: A bug in the greedy loop must be fixed in three places.
Future headroom or charge-formula changes must be synced across all three functions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `def _greedy_pack_bins(items, *, fixed_tokens, charge_fn, limit): ...` returning `(bins, oversized)` and have both new functions call it with their specific charge lambdas.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `prerequisite_fidelity_report` needs to be a separate named function in `parity.py`, or whether `container_fidelity_report` can be generalised with an `attribution_fn` parameter and reused for prerequisite attribution scoring.
evidence: `container_fidelity_report` (parity.py:191): calls `parity_report`, reads `attribution_accuracy`, adds a floor check — same top-level structure

Plan: '`prerequisite_fidelity_report` calls `parity_report(baseline, candidate, min_gold=min_gold)`, reads candidate recall and false acceptance using `_recall_false_accept`, and adds failures when...'
Only one named call-site at ship: `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval`
Rule-of-Three: 1 call-site is premature for a named module-level abstraction in a shared `parity.py`
findings.finding: `prerequisite_fidelity_report` is defined as a named function in `parity.py` with exactly one planned call-site (`prerequisite_packing_spot_eval`), violating Rule-of-Three. It also partially rebuilds `container_fidelity_report`: both call `parity_report`, both read `_recall_false_accept`, both add attribution-accuracy checks. The prerequisite version merely substitutes `pred_prerequisite_id == gold_prerequisite_id` for the G3/G4 criterion match.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 3: 'Define `prerequisite_fidelity_report(baseline, candidate, ...)` in `src/rebar/llm/parity.py`' / `src/rebar/llm/parity.py:191`
scenarios: Recall/false-accept floors diverge between container and prerequisite gates when one is updated and the other is not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of extending `_build_binary` with `extra_fields`, override the Binary construction locally inside `plan_review_verification_model` (e.g. a `PlanBinary` subclass or a local `create_model` call that merges the `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field), keeping the shared kernel builder unchanged.
evidence: Current `_build_binary` has no `extra_fields` parameter; it is called only from `_build_verification_output` (verify_models.py:115)
Plan: 'Only `plan_review_verification_model` passes `binary_extra_fields=...`; Base and code-review callers omit the keyword and remain byte-identical'
One consumer at ship time — Rule-of-Three not satisfied for the new keyword parameter
`plan_review_verification_model` already builds its own `PlanSeverityAttrs` subclass inside its own function body; the same pattern (local overrides) is already the established idiom
findings.finding: The `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields=...)` parametric extension to the shared kernel builder has exactly one planned consumer (`plan_review_verification_model`) at implementation time, violating Rule-of-Three. The `extra_fields` keyword argument is a premature abstraction: the same outcome (adding `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to only the plan-review binary shape) can be achieved without mutating the shared kernel builder.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Change `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None) -> type`' / `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:91`
scenarios: Adding a plan-review-only field to a shared kernel builder couples the kernel to one gate's attribution semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: In `plan_review_verification_model`, after calling `_build_binary(forbid)` to get `Binary`, build a `PlanBinary = create_model('PlanBinary', __base__=Binary, prerequisite_attribution_valid=(str, Field(default='na', ...)))` and pass that to `_build_verification_output`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Simplify the prerequisite-scoped `mint_finding_id` encoding: extend the existing pipe-delimited basis with `'|prerequisite:' + prerequisite_id` before hashing, rather than introducing a bespoke 4-byte big-endian TLV format.
evidence: Existing `mint_finding_id` (orchestrator.py:332): `basis = finding_text + '|' + ','.join(sorted(criteria)); sha256(basis)`
Plan: 'encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length' — custom TLV framing
AC only requires: 'Same-text findings from different prerequisite IDs retain distinct finding IDs'
That AC is trivially satisfied by appending `'|' + prerequisite_id` to the existing `basis` string before hashing
No interoperability, storage, or collision-resistance requirement is cited that would motivate TLV framing
findings.finding: The 4-byte big-endian length-prefixed TLV SHA-256 encoding for prerequisite-scoped `mint_finding_id` is bespoke binary serialization with no stated justification over a simpler extension. The existing encoding (`finding_text + '|' + ','.join(sorted(criteria))`) already achieves stable uniqueness via SHA-256; adding `'|' + prerequisite_id` to the existing basis string would satisfy AC 'same-text findings from different prerequisites retain distinct finding IDs' without introducing a new wire format. The plan cites no collision-resistance requirement that the simple append cannot satisfy.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests: 'encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length, and concatenate...' / `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:332`
scenarios: Implementer must write and test a custom struct-pack encoder; test failure modes differ from the pipe-delimited path.
Future maintainers must understand two ID encoding schemes in the same function.
findings.suggested_fix: In `mint_finding_id`, when `prerequisite_id` is present: `basis = finding_text + '|' + ','.join(sorted(criteria)) + '|prerequisite:' + prerequisite_id`; SHA-256 and truncate as before.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` to the plan's file impact list (explicit kernel edit target named in the Binding criterion section).
evidence: 'Explicit kernel edit target: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py implements the exact _build_binary(..., extra_fields=...) and _build_verification_output(..., binary_extra_fields=...) signatures'
File impact list does not include src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py
File confirmed to exist at src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py with _build_binary(forbid: Any) currently taking only one argument
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` as the explicit kernel edit target (_build_binary and _build_verification_output signature changes with extra_fields) in the 'Binding criterion and CLI registration' section, but this file does not appear in the plan's file impact list.
findings.location: Binding criterion and CLI registration section; Implementation section (_build_binary / _build_verification_output signatures)
scenarios: Implementer misses the verify_models.py change; code-review and kernel verification contracts are silently broken by the omission
findings.suggested_fix: Add to file_impact: {path: 'src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py', reason: 'Extend _build_binary and _build_verification_output with extra_fields/binary_extra_fields keyword arguments; only plan_review_verification_model supplies the mapping.'}

src/rebar/llm/parity.py confirmed to exist with ItemRecord at line 41; not listed in file impact

scenarios: Implementer overlooks updating parity.py; prerequisite_fidelity_report is never defined; the fidelity CLI exits with ImportError

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` as a consumer of the `finalize_verdict` behavioral change (3 call sites); confirm each passes no `prerequisite_indeterminate` coverage key so the new branch is a no-op for them.
evidence: gate_dispatch.py calls orchestrator.finalize_verdict at lines 336, 396, 431
These calls pass coverage dicts without prerequisite_indeterminate — the new branch is safely false — but the existing `if blocking:` is being changed to `elif blocking:`, a structural change
gate_dispatch.py is absent from the plan's file impact list
findings.finding: `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` is called by `gate_dispatch.py` at three sites (lines 336, 396, 431) — all outside the plan_review/ directory — but gate_dispatch.py is not listed as a consumer or edit target for the behavioral change that moves `if blocking:` to `elif blocking:` and inserts a prerequisite-indeterminate branch above it.

scenarios: A logic error in the new prerequisite_indeterminate branch could silently affect gate_dispatch's verify-failure and coach-failure recovery paths
A reviewer auditing gate_dispatch against the plan finds no mention of the impact

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` to the file impact list and confirm whether `indeterminate` needs to be declared as an optional property.
evidence: 'Pass indeterminate into plan_review_coach_inputs in all four [sic] YAML call sites'
plan_review_coach_inputs is registered with `input_schema='plan_review_coach_inputs_input'`
src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json exists but is not in the file impact list
The plan adds reclassified indeterminate findings to `coachable` inside this step
findings.finding: The plan adds `indeterminate` as a YAML input to `plan_review_coach_inputs` in both branch arms but does not list `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` in the file impact, yet the registered input schema for this step would need updating to accept the new field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-paragraph positive rationale for the focused-separate-prompt approach to the Implementation section, explaining why prerequisite material must be isolated in dedicated prompts rather than embedded in general finder prompts (e.g. contamination risk to general criteria, prompt-cache stability, independent retry/escalation per prerequisite, or LLM attention dilution at scale).
evidence: The plan specifies 'Define measurable isolation: capture every request and assert that each general finder... contain neither prerequisite canonical IDs nor prerequisite plan text' but gives no reason why this cannot be achieved by a filtering post-step on a combined prompt
ABSENCE of positive rationale — plan never states 'we choose separate prompts because...' or 'embedding in general prompts fails because...'
The plan's own upstream-contract section says '5967 must deliver... pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=...)' — prerequisites are specifically a new concern warranting explanation
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing focused separate prompts (`plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md` / `plan_review_prerequisite_verifier.md`) over the structurally different alternative of embedding prerequisite context in the existing general Pass-1 finder prompts with strict per-output filtering. The plan specifies extensive isolation requirements but never states why a separate-prompt mechanism is preferable to isolation-at-instruction-level within general finder calls (e.g. passing prerequisite blocks in a distinct `PREREQUISITES` section of the general finder system prompt and filtering findings by the `prerequisite-consistency` criterion ID post-call).
findings.location: Implementation section — first three paragraphs describing the finder/verifier mechanism and isolation requirements; 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts'
scenarios: The absence of stated rationale makes it harder for an implementer to correctly scope the isolation tests, since the reason for the boundary is unclear — tests may over-fit to mechanism rather than to the goal.

If the separate-prompt approach proves inadequate (e.g. context-limit issues from large prerequisite blocks in a separate call), the implementer has no documented basis for choosing an alternative, since the plan never stated why the separate-prompt approach was better.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why the existing `mint_finding_id` pipe-delimited approach and the new 4-field TLV-SHA-256 scheme were chosen over stdlib `uuid.uuid5` (namespace-scoped name-based hashing), or adopt `uuid.uuid5` if no concrete reason to deviate exists.
evidence: 'encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length, and concatenate them in this order: fixed version string prerequisite-finding-id-v1, finding text, comma-joined sorted criteria, and authoritative prerequisite ID. Hash the concatenated bytes with SHA-256 and retain the existing ID truncation.'
ABSENCE: no rationale for not using uuid.uuid5 (a stdlib, collision-resistant, namespace-scoped name-based hash ID) or any other prior-art content-addressable ID scheme.
findings.finding: The plan defines a custom 4-field length-prefixed SHA-256 identity encoding (Binding signal-chain and identity tests) for prerequisite-scoped findings without documenting whether the existing `mint_finding_id` pipe-delimited encoding was itself derived from a prior standard (e.g., ULID, UUID-v5 name-based hashing, or content-addressable ID schemes). UUID v5 uses SHA-1 over a namespace+name byte blob, which is semantically identical to what is being built; the plan does not consider or rule out reusing that standard, which is already available in Python's stdlib (`uuid.uuid5`). If the existing encoding is already custom, this extends a custom approach without Rule-of-Three justification.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests (step 5 / Implementation section 'Extend mint_finding_id')
scenarios: A reviewer or future implementor questions why a bespoke TLV-prefixed SHA-256 scheme was invented instead of the stdlib name-based UUID approach, creating maintenance and auditability debt.
If the existing pipe-delimited encoding also lacks a prior-art citation, the project accumulates two independent custom ID schemes with no documented rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Binding signal-chain section, add a one-sentence justification: e.g., 'uuid.uuid5 is not used because it truncates via SHA-1 and does not expose raw SHA-256 bytes needed for the existing ID-truncation contract.' If that justification does not hold, replace the bespoke encoding with `uuid.uuid5(uuid.NAMESPACE_URL, canonical_name_string)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether a single generic `pack_bins(blocks, *, charge_fn, ...)` function in `sizing.py` can serve both finder and verifier cases, and document the reason for two separate functions if the generic approach is rejected.
evidence: 'Define pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], ...) in sizing.py'
'Define pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins(records: Sequence[PrerequisiteVerificationBlock], ...) in sizing.py'
'Use the same fixed-input calculation as finder packing; charge each whole block ...'
ABSENCE: no mention of whether the existing general packing function signature was evaluated for extension via a generic Block protocol/ABC before defining two new packing functions.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a parallel prerequisite-packing and bin-splitting infrastructure (`pack_prerequisite_bins`, `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins`, `PrerequisiteBlock`, `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock`) in `sizing.py` that mirrors the general finder/verifier packing already present there, but the plan never checks whether the existing general packing functions could be parameterised or extended to cover the prerequisite case rather than duplicating the architecture. This is a potential Rule-of-Three violation: two near-identical packing abstractions in the same file without documented justification for why a unified generic packer was rejected.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Size the actual focused prompt topology... through a prerequisite-specific adapter in sizing.py') and Binding workflow production section ('pack_prerequisite_bins', 'pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins')
scenarios: A third packing case (e.g., parent/child context batching in a later story) adds a third near-identical function, cementing an anti-pattern.
Divergent maintenance: a bug fix to the charge calculation in one packer is not propagated to the others.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `ParityReport` (and `parity.ItemRecord`) supports the proposed new fields without breaking existing callers, or explicitly specify the extension mechanism (subclass, composition, TypedDict with total=False, etc.).
evidence: 'Its metrics add coverage_completeness, prerequisite_attribution_error_rate, and the four configured floors.'
'passed is true only when the base report and every added check pass.'
'Preserve existing fields and container behavior.'
ABSENCE: no verification that the existing ParityReport type (dataclass/TypedDict/dict) is open for extension without breaking existing serialization or comparison logic.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a dedicated `prerequisite_fidelity_report` function and opt-in `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` in `parity.py` / `fidelity_spot_eval.py` alongside the existing `parity_report` and `packing_spot_eval`. The plan states it calls `parity_report` internally, but also adds four new metric keys (`coverage_completeness`, `prerequisite_attribution_error_rate`, `min_recall`, `max_false_accept`) to a `ParityReport`. It is not verified whether the existing `ParityReport` structure (likely a dataclass or TypedDict) actually supports extension with new keys, or whether extending it would break existing callers that pattern-match or serialize the report. No prior-art for this extension pattern (e.g., subclassing, composition, `extra` fields) is cited.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 3 and 'Add optional gold_prerequisite_id and pred_prerequisite_id fields to parity.ItemRecord'
scenarios: Existing tests that assert the exact set of keys in a ParityReport dict or dataclass fail after new metric keys are added.
The CLI that prints metric keys emits unexpected keys, breaking downstream parse scripts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a schema-level or runner-level enforcement that distinguishes focused verifier calls from general verifier calls, such as a separate focused-verifier contract that makes `prerequisite_attribution_valid` a required non-`na` field, or add a runner-side coercion that maps `na` to `no` on the focused path and documents this as the protocol.
evidence: The plan says `only plan_review_verification_model passes binary_extra_fields={"prerequisite_attribution_valid": (str, Field(default="na", ...))}` and `the focused decide path reads verif["binary"].get("prerequisite_attribution_valid", "na") as a veto outside validity arithmetic`
Binding verifier prompt: 'set `prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes` only when … `no` when … and never `na` for a focused entry'

The prohibition is expressed only as a prompt instruction, not as a schema-level constraint; an executor that omits or overrides the focused verifier prompt gets `na` silently, which then converts to `attribution-invalid` indeterminate — the same outcome as a correct `no`, but through a different code path than specified
The existing `_build_binary` function (verify_models.py line 91) builds a flat model; no per-call-context differentiation exists or is planned at the schema level
findings.finding: `prerequisite_attribution_valid` defaults to `"na"` in the shared `plan_review_verification` schema, but the focused verifier prompt is the sole enforcement point prohibiting `na` for focused entries — the schema itself cannot distinguish focused from general verifier calls.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'only the two focused prerequisite prompts may receive prerequisite material'; Binding prerequisite block and verifier prompt: 'never `na` for focused entries'; `verify_models.py` `_build_binary` (not yet modified)
scenarios: Implementer reuses the general verifier prompt for the focused verifier path by mistake; all focused findings get `na` and are converted to `attribution-invalid` indeterminate rather than being evaluated on merit

A focused verifier LLM outputs `na` for a valid attribution because the prohibition is only in the prompt, not the schema; the decide path silently converts the entire prerequisite record to indeterminate without surfacing the root cause
findings.suggested_fix: Define a separate `plan_review_prerequisite_verification_model` that sets `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with `default='no'` (fail-closed) instead of `'na'`, and registers it as a distinct contract used only by the focused verifier path. This eliminates the prompt-only enforcement and co-locates the constraint with the schema that needs it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"indeterminate": { "type": "array", "description": "Indeterminate findings from plan_review_decide, including prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate reclassified blockers." }` to `plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` properties.
evidence: The existing `plan_review_coach_inputs_input` schema (verified) has `required: ["surviving"]` and `additionalProperties: true`, with no `indeterminate` property declared
The plan says to pass `indeterminate` in all four YAML call sites but does not add `indeterminate` to the schema's `properties` object

The Binding criterion and CLI registration section says 'Extend `plan_review_coach_inputs` coachable inputs with indeterminate findings whose reason is `prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`' but still does not name the schema file
The existing `plan_review_coach_inputs` implementation at workflow_ops.py line 383-403 only accesses `surviving` and `blocking` from `ctx.inputs` — `indeterminate` is not read
findings.finding: The `plan_review_coach_inputs` step receives a new `indeterminate` input to extract `reclassified` findings, but the `plan_review_coach_inputs_input` schema is not updated to declare this field — creating a schema/protocol mismatch.
findings.location: Binding workflow production and verifier batching: 'Pass `indeterminate` into `plan_review_coach_inputs` in all four YAML call sites. In `plan_review_coach_inputs`, compute `reclassified = [f for f in indeterminate if f.get("reason") == "prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate"]`'; `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json`
scenarios: The `plan_review_coach_inputs_input` schema golden test (`test_workflow_schema_golden.py`) fails because the schema doesn't declare the new `indeterminate` field that the code now reads
A workflow validation step that enforces `additionalProperties: false` in the future would reject the `indeterminate` field silently passed by the YAML, since it's undeclared
findings.suggested_fix: Add the `indeterminate` property to `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` alongside the existing `surviving` and `blocking` properties.

findings.finding: AC 'Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results' has no concrete proving command in the Verification section; only a general `pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` run is listed, which does not demonstrate that the container-corpus fidelity baseline numbers are unchanged after `parity.ItemRecord` is extended.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results'; Verification section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Verification: `Run .venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --container-corpus --baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-container.baseline.json; require exit 0 and no regression in recall or false-acceptance relative to the pinned baseline.`

findings.finding: The plan's description of `prerequisite_attribution_valid` as an 'extra-binary-fields argument' to `_build_verification_output` is ambiguous about whether the field lives inside the `Binary` sub-model or at the `Verification` level. The existing `_build_binary` builds the `Binary` type from `GRADED_BINARY` (a tuple); the plan explicitly says do NOT add the field to `GRADED_BINARY` or `_build_binary`. If the implementer adds it to `Binary` anyway (via the 'extra-binary-fields' argument path through `_build_binary`), it will be excluded from `validity()` scoring (correct) but the model's `extra='ignore'` posture means a strict-mode verifier could silently drop it. If it lives at the `Verification` level (alongside `binary`, not inside it), the decide step reads it as `verif['prerequisite_attribution_valid']` rather than `verif['binary']['prerequisite_attribution_valid']`. The plan's language 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2' and 'only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`' implies the field is added to the `Verification` record, not to `Binary`—but 'extra-binary-fields argument' strongly implies it goes inside `Binary`. This ambiguity could cause the decide step to look for the field in the wrong location, making `no`/`na` attribution detection silently fail.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract… Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument'; Binding criterion and CLI registration section: 'Explicit kernel edit target: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` adds optional extra binary fields to `_build_verification_output`'; also verify_models.py `_build_verification_output` at line 108
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'Place `prerequisite_attribution_valid` as a direct field on the `Verification` record—sibling to `binary`, not inside it—so the decide step reads `verif.get("prerequisite_attribution_valid", "na")` and the existing `validity()` loop over `GRADED_BINARY` is untouched.'

findings.finding: `_build_verification_output`'s new optional extra-binary-fields argument has no defined type signature or calling convention, leaving the co-location between the schema modification and its processing protocol absent.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract... extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument'; also `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: '`_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls, *, extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None) -> type` — when `extra_fields` is `None` (the default) the output model is byte-identical to today; each key in `extra_fields` is appended as an additional field on the `Verification` model via `create_model`'s keyword-field syntax. `plan_review_verification_model` passes `extra_fields={"prerequisite_attribution_valid": (str, Field(default="na", description="yes|no|na"))}`. Other callers pass nothing.'

findings.finding: The plan extends `plan_review_coach` coachable inputs with indeterminate findings whose reason is `prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, but provides no processing protocol specifying WHERE in the coach op the distinction between coachable and non-coachable indeterminate items is made.
findings.location: Binding criterion and CLI registration section: 'Extend `plan_review_coach` coachable inputs with indeterminate findings whose reason is `prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, so reclassified blockers retain remediation notes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'In `plan_review_decide`, when copying entries from `parts["blocking"]` to `parts["indeterminate"]`, set `reason="prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate"` on each copied finding. In `plan_review_coach`, include `indeterminate` findings with `reason=="prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate"` in the `coachable` list alongside blocking and surfaced findings.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify which workflow step produces the `prerequisite_coverage` array that must be wired into all four terminal `plan_review_coach` call sites in the YAML, leaving the YAML wiring for the focused domain underspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps and arrays through both branches in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`'; `plan_review_coach` section: 'build the existing local `coverage` dictionary with a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` key computed as `any(record.disposition == "indeterminate")` from its `prerequisite_coverage` input'

findings.finding: The focused verifier batching algorithm is not specified. The plan fully defines `pack_prerequisite_bins` for the focused finder path (Binding step 4, with full function signature, inputs, outputs, and greedy algorithm), but for the focused verifier side it only states 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget' and 'Focused verifier context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm as the finder: split the stable finding-record list into contiguous halves'. No packing function, sizing adapter call, token budget computation, or function name is given for grouping verifier finding-records into calls. The plan says to use a 'prerequisite-specific adapter in `sizing.py`' for finder sizing but doesn't state whether that same adapter or a separate one handles verifier sizing.
findings.location: Implementation section — verifier batching paragraph ('Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget'); Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — verifier context-limit recovery

findings.finding: The routing of `pack_prerequisite_bins`'s oversized-block list to `indeterminate/evaluation-error` is stated but the intermediate path (singleton escalation vs. direct error) is not specified, leaving the failure fallback for oversized blocks ambiguous.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'On context-limit error, split a stable bin into contiguous halves recursively. Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Largest-window exhaustion emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large`'; also `pack_prerequisite_bins` spec in Binding implementation sequence step 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'Oversized blocks returned by `pack_prerequisite_bins` are treated as singleton bins and passed through the same model-escalation ladder as runtime context-limit errors; only when every model in `models_at_or_above()` returns a context-limit error does the block receive `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the '4 YAML call sites' claim to '2 YAML call sites' — plan-review.yaml has exactly 2 plan_review_coach_inputs steps (one per verify branch arm).
evidence: 'Pass indeterminate into plan_review_coach_inputs in all four YAML call sites'
plan-review.yaml has exactly two `uses: plan_review_coach_inputs` steps: line 179 in the agentic verify arm and line 259 in the single-turn verify arm
The passthrough branch (else of the top-level branch) has no coach_inputs step at all
findings.finding: The plan states 'Pass indeterminate into plan_review_coach_inputs in all four YAML call sites' but plan-review.yaml contains exactly 2 plan_review_coach_inputs steps (lines 179 and 259 — one per verify branch arm). The count is factually wrong.
findings.location: Binding workflow production and verifier batching section; plan-review.yaml lines 179, 259
scenarios: Implementer searches for non-existent third/fourth sites, wastes time, or incorrectly introduces extra steps
Structural confusion leads to incorrect YAML edits that break the workflow
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'all four YAML call sites' with 'both YAML call sites' in the Binding workflow production section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'readable state' predicate for a `PlanRelationSnapshot` prerequisite entry — e.g., specify which field on the snapshot record signals readability and what value constitutes unreadable (such as a null/absent plan text, or a specific `readable: bool` field name from `75de`'s contract).
evidence: The upstream contract section says `75de` must deliver a `PlanRelationSnapshot` 'keyed by authoritative canonical ID with relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and plan text'
The criterion gating says 'readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites'
Neither section defines 'readable' — it appears to mean 'has plan text accessible from the snapshot' but this is inferred, not stated
ABSENCE: no explicit definition of `readable_state` as a boolean field or condition in the `PlanRelationSnapshot` contract
findings.finding: The 'readable state' gating condition for the prerequisite-consistency criterion is never defined — the plan uses the phrase 'contains readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites' without specifying what makes a prerequisite readable vs. unreadable.
findings.location: Implementation section first bullet: 'It runs only when the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload succeeded and contains readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites'
scenarios: A prerequisite ticket exists in the relation snapshot but its plan text could not be fetched (deleted ticket, permission error, network failure during snapshot collection) — is it 'readable'? The plan doesn't say
An implementer implements `readable` as 'has non-empty plan text' while another implements it as 'did not error during fetch' — the criterion activation boundary differs between the two

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name at least one test (e.g. `test_batching_never_splits_subject_or_prerequisite_block` and `test_focused_general_index_domains_independent`) for AC4 and AC7 respectively, and add `pytest -k` proving commands for each in the Verification section.
evidence: AC4: 'Stable focused batching never splits the subject or a prerequisite block' — no test name, no pytest -k command
AC7: 'General and focused index domains are independently reshaped and cannot shift each other' — no test name, no pytest -k command

By contrast, AC12 maps to named tests `test_prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain_agentic` and AC13 maps to `test_largest_window_exhaustion_emits_indeterminate`, each with explicit proving commands.
The plan's Verification section names the test files but gives no `-k` filter or test name for these two ACs.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Stable focused batching never splits the subject or a prerequisite block') and AC7 ('General and focused index domains are independently reshaped and cannot shift each other') have no named test or explicit proving command mapping them to the test suite. Every other AC with a structural invariant (AC12, AC13) is mapped to a named test with a `pytest -k` command, but AC4 and AC7 are not.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, AC lines 4 and 7
scenarios: An executor completing the implementation has no concrete target test to write for AC4 and AC7 — a reviewer cannot confirm these invariants are verified without hunting through the unnamed test suite.
A CI run passing `test_plan_review_prerequisites.py` does not prove these specific invariants are tested if the author forgot to add them.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Binding focused prompt and failure contracts (or Verification section): 'Add `test_batching_never_splits_subject_or_prerequisite_block` asserting that `pack_prerequisite_bins` output contains no bin where a subject token or a prerequisite block appears truncated; add `test_focused_general_index_domains_independent` asserting that reshaping focused findings at indices 0..P-1 does not alter general finding indices 0..G-1. Run `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py -k "never_splits or index_domains_independent"`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the module for `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` and `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` (likely `sizing.py` to match `PrerequisiteBlock` and `pack_prerequisite_bins`).
evidence: The Binding prerequisite block section explicitly places `PrerequisiteBlock` in `sizing.py`
The Binding workflow section introduces `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` and its associated packing function `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` without stating the file
By contrast, every other new type in the plan has an explicit module: `_prerequisite_coverage_model` → `passes.py`, `PrerequisiteBlock` → `sizing.py`, `prerequisite_fidelity_report` → `parity.py`
ABSENCE: no 'in `sizing.py`' or similar phrase accompanies `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` in the Binding workflow section
findings.finding: `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` has no explicit module placement — unlike `PrerequisiteBlock` (explicitly placed in `sizing.py`), the Binding workflow section introduces this dataclass without naming its target module.
findings.location: Binding workflow production and verifier batching section: 'Define `@dataclass(frozen=True) class PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` with `canonical_id: str`, `findings: tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]`, and `rendered_text: str`'
scenarios: An implementer places `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` in `passes.py` while another places `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins` in `sizing.py`, creating an import cycle (sizing → passes → sizing is already the existing pattern)
Different implementations end up with two different modules owning types that are intended to be co-located, causing confusion at the sizing/packing seam
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'in `sizing.py`' to the `PrerequisiteVerificationBlock` definition sentence in Binding workflow production and verifier batching, matching the pattern of the `PrerequisiteBlock` definition.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
